outline.title: Frontend Engineering Onboarding: Advanced React & Modern Standards
outline.description: A comprehensive onboarding program designed for newly hired frontend engineers to align with our specific React implementation, architectural patterns, and modern frontend standards. This course covers monorepo navigation, advanced component design, state management, testing paradigms, and performance optimization to ensure high-quality product delivery.

modules.title: Development Environment & Architecture
learning_objectives: Navigate the internal monorepo structure efficiently
Set up the local development environment and tooling
Apply standard styling configurations and design system tokens

modules.title: Advanced React Patterns & State Management
learning_objectives: Architect scalable components using functional patterns
Design custom React hooks for reusable logic
Implement local and global state management strategies

lessons.title: State Management: Local vs. Global Solutions

modules.title: Data Fetching & API Integration
learning_objectives: Integrate APIs with strict TypeScript typing

modules.title: Testing Paradigms & Quality Assurance
learning_objectives: Write robust unit tests using Jest
Implement integration tests with React Testing Library
Ensure WCAG accessibility standards in components

lessons.title: Integration Testing with React Testing Library

modules.title: Performance Tuning & Code Delivery
learning_objectives: Profile and optimize React rendering performance
Navigate the team's code review process
Utilize CI/CD pipelines for seamless deployment

lessons.title: Monorepo Navigation & Architecture

slide_bullets: Workspace-based architecture using Turborepo
Separation of concerns: /apps vs. /packages
Shared configurations for TypeScript, ESLint, and Tailwind
segments.narration: Welcome to our engineering team. Our codebase is organized as a monorepo managed by Turborepo. Inside, you will find two main directories: 'apps', which contains our deployable applications, and 'packages', which houses shared libraries such as our design system, custom hooks, and shared TypeScript configurations. This structure ensures code reusability and consistent tooling across all frontend projects.

key_takeaways: The monorepo separates deployable applications in /apps from reusable libraries in /packages.
Turborepo is used to orchestrate builds, linting, and testing across workspaces.

lessons.title: Local Environment Setup & Tooling

slide_bullets: Node.js version management using Volta or .nvmrc
Package management with pnpm for speed and disk efficiency
Pre-commit hooks with Husky and lint-staged
segments.narration: To set up your local environment, ensure you are using the correct Node.js version specified in our .nvmrc file. We use pnpm as our package manager to guarantee fast, deterministic installations. Upon committing code, Husky will automatically run lint-staged to execute Prettier and ESLint, ensuring all code meets our quality standards before reaching remote branches.

key_takeaways: Always use the designated Node.js version and pnpm for package management.
Husky pre-commit hooks enforce code formatting and linting automatically.

lessons.title: Styling Standards & Design System Integration

slide_bullets: Tailwind CSS integrated with our shared design tokens
Strict adherence to the semantic color palette
Utility-first styling with custom component extensions
segments.narration: Our styling architecture relies on Tailwind CSS, configured globally via our shared design system package. When building UI components, always use our semantic design tokens for colors, spacing, and typography. Avoid hardcoding arbitrary values; instead, extend Tailwind's configuration if a specific custom value is required by the design specifications.

key_takeaways: Tailwind CSS utility classes must align with our shared design tokens.
Avoid arbitrary values in Tailwind; use semantic tokens to maintain design consistency.

lessons.title: Component Design & Composition Patterns

slide_bullets: Favor composition over deeply nested prop drilling
Implement compound components for complex UI controls
Maintain clean, readable JSX structures
segments.narration: To build scalable React interfaces, we favor component composition. For complex UI controls like dropdowns or tabs, use the Compound Component pattern. This allows state to be shared implicitly among child components using React Context, resulting in highly flexible and readable JSX without polluting your components with unnecessary props.

key_takeaways: Use compound components to manage implicit state in complex UI controls.
Composition reduces prop drilling and enhances component flexibility.

lessons.title: Custom Hooks & Logic Extraction

slide_bullets: Isolate business logic from presentational components
Create reusable custom hooks for side effects and state
Adhere to the Rules of Hooks and clean up side effects
segments.narration: Keep your UI components thin by extracting business logic, state management, and side effects into custom React hooks. This separation of concerns makes your components purely presentational and highly testable. When writing custom hooks, always ensure proper cleanup of event listeners, subscriptions, or timers to prevent memory leaks.

key_takeaways: Custom hooks isolate business logic, leaving components focused on rendering.
Always implement cleanup functions within hooks to prevent memory leaks.

lessons.title: State Management: Local vs. Global Solutions

slide_bullets: Use local useState and useReducer for component-specific state
Leverage Zustand for lightweight, global client-side state
Avoid overusing React Context for high-frequency updates
segments.narration: Choosing the correct state management tool is critical. For component-specific state, rely on standard useState or useReducer hooks. For global, client-side state, we use Zustand due to its simplicity and performance. Avoid using React Context for high-frequency state updates, as it can trigger unnecessary re-renders across the entire component tree.

key_takeaways: Keep state as local as possible to optimize rendering performance.
Use Zustand for global client-side state, and reserve React Context for low-frequency updates.

lessons.title: Data Fetching & Caching Strategies

slide_bullets: Manage server state using TanStack Query (React Query)
Configure staleTime and cacheTime to optimize network requests
Implement optimistic updates for responsive user experiences
segments.narration: We manage all server state using TanStack Query. This library handles caching, background updates, and stale data out of the box. Be sure to configure appropriate staleTime and cacheTime values based on how dynamic the data is. For mutations, implement optimistic updates to make the user interface feel instantaneous.

key_takeaways: TanStack Query is our standard for managing, caching, and syncing server state.
Optimistic updates improve perceived performance during data mutations.

lessons.title: API Integration & Error Handling

slide_bullets: Standardized Axios client with global interceptors
Graceful error handling using React Error Boundaries
Structured fallback UIs for network failures
segments.narration: All network requests go through our standardized Axios client, which automatically injects authentication tokens and handles global error logging. To prevent application crashes during network or runtime failures, wrap your route components in React Error Boundaries to display user-friendly fallback interfaces.

key_takeaways: Axios interceptors handle global request and response concerns.
Error Boundaries isolate runtime failures and preserve application stability.

lessons.title: TypeScript Typing for API Contracts

slide_bullets: Generate TypeScript interfaces from OpenAPI schemas
Validate runtime payloads using Zod schemas
Avoid using 'any' to preserve type safety across boundaries
segments.narration: To maintain strict type safety, we generate TypeScript interfaces directly from our backend OpenAPI schemas. For dynamic runtime data validation, we use Zod. This ensures that the data entering our application matches our expected types, eliminating runtime errors caused by unexpected API contract changes.

key_takeaways: Generate TypeScript types from backend schemas to ensure contract alignment.
Use Zod to validate external API payloads at the application boundary.

lessons.title: Unit Testing with Jest

slide_bullets: Use Jest for testing pure functions, utilities, and hooks
Mock external dependencies and modules cleanly
Focus on behavior and edge cases rather than implementation details
segments.narration: We use Jest to write unit tests for pure functions, utility modules, and custom hooks. When writing unit tests, focus on testing the behavior and edge cases of your code. Mock external dependencies, such as network clients or global stores, to keep your unit tests isolated, fast, and reliable.

key_takeaways: Jest is our primary runner for unit testing pure logic and utilities.
Mock external modules to keep unit tests fast and isolated.

lessons.title: Integration Testing with React Testing Library

segments.slide_title: Testing Components as Users See Them
slide_bullets: Query elements using accessible roles (getByRole)
Simulate realistic user interactions with userEvent
Avoid testing internal state or private component methods
segments.narration: For component integration testing, we use React Testing Library. This library encourages testing components from the user's perspective. Always prioritize querying elements by their accessible roles, such as 'button' or 'textbox', and use the userEvent library to simulate realistic interactions like typing and clicking.

key_takeaways: React Testing Library tests component behavior, not implementation details.
Query elements by accessible roles to ensure tests align with real-world usage.

lessons.title: Testing for Accessibility (a11y)

slide_bullets: Integrate jest-axe for automated accessibility checks
Ensure proper semantic HTML and ARIA attributes
Verify keyboard navigation and focus management
segments.narration: Accessibility is a core requirement for our products. We use jest-axe to run automated accessibility audits on our components during integration testing. Additionally, ensure your components use semantic HTML, manage focus correctly when modals or menus open, and support full keyboard navigation.

key_takeaways: Automate accessibility testing using jest-axe in your test suites.
Semantic HTML and robust focus management are essential for keyboard navigation.

lessons.title: Performance Profiling & Optimization

slide_bullets: Identify unnecessary re-renders using React DevTools Profiler
Memoize expensive computations with useMemo and useCallback
Implement code splitting with React.lazy and Suspense
segments.narration: Performance issues should be diagnosed using the React DevTools Profiler. Use useMemo and useCallback to memoize expensive computations and stable callback references only when profiling indicates a bottleneck. To improve initial load times, implement code splitting using React.lazy and Suspense for route-level components.

key_takeaways: Profile components before applying memoization optimizations.
Use React.lazy and Suspense to split code and reduce initial bundle sizes.

lessons.title: Code Review Standards & Best Practices

slide_bullets: Keep Pull Requests small, focused, and atomic
Adhere to Conventional Commits for clear history
Provide constructive, actionable feedback in reviews
segments.narration: Our code review process is designed to maintain high quality and share knowledge. Keep your Pull Requests small and focused on a single task. We follow the Conventional Commits standard for commit messages. When reviewing peer code, focus on architecture, readability, and edge cases, keeping feedback constructive and actionable.

key_takeaways: Small, atomic Pull Requests speed up reviews and reduce integration bugs.
Conventional Commits are required to maintain a clean, automated changelog.

lessons.title: CI/CD Pipelines & Deployment Workflows

slide_bullets: Automated linting, formatting, and testing on every push
Preview deployments generated automatically for PRs
Production deployments triggered via main branch merges
segments.narration: Our CI/CD pipeline, built on GitHub Actions, automatically runs linting, formatting, and unit tests on every push. Every Pull Request generates an isolated preview deployment, allowing designers and product managers to review changes. Once merged into the main branch, our pipeline automatically deploys the updates to production.

key_takeaways: CI pipelines enforce quality checks automatically before code can be merged.
Preview deployments facilitate collaborative QA and design reviews.

questions.question: Where are reusable packages and shared configurations located in our monorepo?
questions.explanation: Our monorepo structure separates deployable applications in /apps and reusable libraries, design tokens, and shared configurations in /packages.

questions.question: Which tool is used to enforce code formatting and linting during the pre-commit stage?
questions.explanation: Husky and lint-staged work together to run ESLint and Prettier on staged files before a commit is finalized.

questions.question: What pattern is recommended for managing implicit state among a group of related UI components?
questions.explanation: The Compound Components pattern allows parent and child components to share state implicitly using React Context, making the JSX clean and flexible.

questions.question: Why is Zustand preferred over React Context for high-frequency global state updates?
options: React Context can trigger unnecessary re-renders across the entire component tree.
Zustand is built directly into the React core library.
React Context cannot handle asynchronous actions.
questions.explanation: React Context can cause performance bottlenecks because any update to the context value forces all consuming components to re-render. Zustand avoids this with selector-based subscriptions.

questions.question: Which library is our standard for managing and caching server state?
questions.explanation: TanStack Query (formerly React Query) is our standard library for fetching, caching, and synchronizing server state.

questions.question: How do we validate external API payloads at the application boundary?
options: Using Zod schemas for runtime validation
questions.explanation: We use Zod schemas to perform runtime validation on API payloads, ensuring that unexpected backend changes do not cause silent frontend failures.

questions.question: What is the preferred way to query elements when writing integration tests with React Testing Library?
options: By querying CSS class names directly
By using internal component state values
questions.explanation: React Testing Library recommends querying elements by their accessible roles to mimic how assistive technologies and real users interact with the UI.

questions.question: Which tool do we use to automate accessibility audits during integration testing?
questions.explanation: We integrate jest-axe into our testing suite to run automated accessibility checks and catch common WCAG violations.

questions.question: What should you do before applying optimization techniques like useMemo or useCallback?
options: Apply them to every component by default
Profile the application using React DevTools to identify actual bottlenecks
Disable CI/CD pipelines to speed up local builds
Convert all components into class components
questions.explanation: Premature optimization can complicate code. You should always profile the application first to ensure memoization is targeting an actual performance bottleneck.

questions.question: What commit standard do we follow to maintain structured and automated changelogs?
questions.explanation: We follow the Conventional Commits standard to ensure our commit history is structured, readable, and compatible with automated release tooling.

cards.back: The build system used to orchestrate tasks, caching, and workspace execution in our monorepo.
Our fast, disk-efficient package manager that uses a content-addressable store and hard links.
Visual design atoms (colors, spacing, typography) defined in our shared package and integrated with Tailwind CSS.
A design pattern where components work together to manage state implicitly, avoiding prop drilling.
A lightweight, fast, and selector-based state management library used for global client-side state.
Functions starting with 'use' that allow you to extract and share stateful logic across components.
Our standard library for fetching, caching, synchronizing, and updating server state in React.
A TypeScript-first schema declaration and validation library used to validate API payloads at runtime.
A React component that catches JavaScript errors anywhere in its child component tree and displays a fallback UI.
A testing utility focused on testing React components from the user's perspective without relying on implementation details.
A library used to run automated accessibility (a11y) audits within Jest tests.
The preferred React Testing Library query that searches for elements within the accessibility tree.
A function that lets you render a dynamic import as a regular component, enabling code splitting.
A specification for adding human and machine-readable meaning to commit messages.
An isolated, ephemeral deployment generated automatically for each Pull Request to facilitate QA and review.
